beats.narration: When steel gets inches thick, X ray tubes and even cobalt sixty fall short. Megavolt machines take over.

payload.title: MEGAVOLT MACHINES
payload.sub: Four ways to push electrons to millions of volts.

beats.narration: Meet four machines: the resonance transformer's tuned coil, the Van de Graaff's charged belt, the microwave-driven linac, and the betatron's circular orbit.

payload.heading: Four routes to millions of volts

beats.narration: The linac surfs electrons on microwaves down a waveguide — huge output, short exposures. The betatron whirls them in a magnetic ring — tiny spot, sharp image.
Output is rated at one meter in coulombs per kilogram of air. They run remotely in interlocked vaults; filtration now shapes beams and absorbs scatter.
So remember the trade: need speed on thick steel, pick the linac. Need the sharpest image, the betatron earns its longer exposure.

payload.sub: Choose by what the part needs most.
